title: Mannaha Jiṇāṇaṁ Sajjhāy (The Lay Devotee's Daily Duties)
summary: This is a short Prakrit study-hymn (sajjhāy) that lists, in five compact verses, the everyday duties of a Jain lay devotee (śrāvaka). It moves from the foundation of right faith, through daily worship, charity, self-restraint and study, to one's duties toward fellow Jains, the community, and the scriptures. It is recited so that a householder is reminded — every single day — of the full shape of a devout life, all to be lived under the guidance of a true teacher.
ritualPlacement: Recited in the Daivasika (Devasi) pratikramaṇa, in the closing study-portion (svādhyāya), as the third member of the Devasi sajjhāy cluster — after Drumapuṣpikā and Pañca-Parameṣṭhi — following Muni Vandanā and before the sāmāyika is concluded (canonical order 18). A sajjhāy is sacred-study recitation and is NOT one of the six Āvaśyakas. Because it belongs specifically to the Devasi study-portion, it is not part of the shared core-sūtra set that the Pakkhī/Cāumāsī/Sāṁvatsarī reuse with word-substitution; that substitution rule does not apply here.
authorAndSource: A traditional anonymous Prakrit gāthā-cluster enumerating the śrāvaka's daily duties; it circulates widely in Śvetāmbara lay liturgy and is printed in this book among the Devasi sajjhāys. No individual author is named in the source book, and accessible scholarship does not fix a single ancient composer — later printed editions carry commentaries (e.g. a Prabodha-dīpikā vṛtti) rather than original attribution. Treat authorship as not firmly attested (see notes).
structureArc: The five verses lay out the duties roughly in widening circles. v.1 sets the doctrinal footing: accept the Jina's command (āṇā), abandon false belief (mithyātva), hold right faith (samyaktva), and be diligent each day in the six Āvaśyakas. v.2 adds periodic and personal observances: the poṣadha vow on holy days, plus dāna, śīla, tapas, bhāva, study (svādhyāya), the Namaskāra, beneficence (paropakāra) and careful restraint (jayaṇā). v.3 turns to devotional and social acts: worship and praise of the Jina, praise of the guru, affection for co-religionists (sādharmika-vātsalya), honesty in dealings (vyavahāra-śuddhi), and the chariot- and pilgrimage-processions (ratha-yātrā, tīrtha-yātrā). v.4 cultivates the inner life: calm (upaśama), discernment (viveka), restraint of karmic inflow (saṁvara), carefulness of speech (bhāṣā-samiti), compassion to the six classes of life, keeping company with the pious, sense-control (karaṇa-dama) and a conduct-bent mind (caraṇa-pariṇāma). v.5 closes with duties toward the larger tradition — high regard for the Saṅgha, copying/preserving scriptures (pustaka-lekhana), and glorifying the tīrtha (prabhāvanā) — sealing all of them as the constant duty of devotees, to be done daily by the instruction of a true guru (suguru-upadeśa).
recurringImagery: This sajjhāy is enumerative rather than metaphorical: it has no extended figurative image running across the verses. Its one unifying device is the catalogue itself — the well-known list of the śrāvaka's daily duties (traditionally counted as thirty-six) — bound together by the closing refrain of v.5, that all of these are 'the constant duty of devotees, [performed] always by the instruction of a good guru.' Each verse simply contributes more items to that single, guru-anchored list.
keyThemes: right faith and rejection of false belief
daily diligence in the six Āvaśyakas
charity, self-restraint, austerity and right disposition
devotion: worship of the Jina and the guru
social and communal duty toward fellow Jains and the Saṅgha
inner cultivation: calm, discernment, restraint, compassion
preservation and glorification of the scriptures and the tīrtha
all duties grounded in the true guru's instruction

glossary.definition: Sacred-study recitation (Skt. svādhyāya); a hymn meant for reflective study, not one of the six Āvaśyakas.
A Jain lay devotee / householder; 'śrāddha' in the verse's saḍḍha; the audience and subject of these duties.
The command or teaching of the Jina (Skt. ājñā); to be accepted as authoritative.
False or deluded belief; its abandonment is the first duty named.
Right faith / correct insight (samyag-darśana); to be firmly held.
The six daily obligatory observances in which the devotee is to be diligent each day.
A periodic fast-and-restraint observance kept on holy days (parva).
The four-fold dharma: charity, ethical conduct, austerity, and inner disposition/intent.
Careful, watchful conduct that avoids harm to living beings (Skt. yatanā).
Affectionate service and support toward co-religionists who share one's faith.
The ceremonial chariot-procession of the Jina image.
Stopping the inflow of karma through restraint.
Carefulness of speech; one of the five samitis (regulations of conduct).
Compassion toward the six classes of living beings (earth-, water-, fire-, air-, plant-bodied, and mobile beings).
Acts that illuminate and glorify the tīrtha / Jain order, raising its esteem in the world.
The instruction of a true, qualified teacher, under which all these duties are to be performed.

howToRead: A newcomer can read this simply as a daily checklist of an ideal Jain householder's life, taking each item at face value and noting how the verses move from faith, to worship, to community, to inner virtue. An advanced practitioner will recognise the cluster as the canonical thirty-six śrāvaka-duties and may map each term to its fuller treatment elsewhere (e.g. the twelve lay vows, the five samitis, the six-fold dharma), reading the closing line as the hermeneutic key that subordinates the whole list to a living guru's guidance.

notes: The source book names no author for this sajjhāy; popular and printed editions treat it as a traditional gāthā-cluster, and accessible scholarship does not firmly attribute it to a single composer — authorship is therefore marked uncertain. The duties are conventionally counted as thirty-six (the chattīsa kṛtya of a śrāvaka), but the exact tally varies by how compound items are split; this preface follows the verses verbatim rather than imposing a count. Orthography (e.g. Prakrit સજઝાય / સજ્ઝાય, છવ્વિહ, રહજત્તા) follows the book's readings verbatim, including its internal spelling variations, and is not normalised.
